meta.case: Brightmarket Retail GmbH  v  Cobalt Cloud Analytics Ltd
meta.theme: B2B SaaS analytics platform / GDPR dispute (EU law) — with seven planted adversarial 'traps' for the verifier

eu_acts.title: Directive (EU) 2019/770 (supply of digital content and services)
Regulation (EU) 2019/1150 (fairness and transparency for business users of online intermediation services)
Commission Implementing Decision (EU) 2021/914 (standard contractual clauses for transfers to third countries)
Commission Decision 2010/87/EU (the 2010 standard contractual clauses, controller-to-processor)
Directive 2002/58/EC (ePrivacy / Privacy and Electronic Communications Directive)
Directive (EU) 2022/2555 (measures for a high common level of cybersecurity — NIS2)
Judgment in Case C-311/18 (Data Protection Commissioner v Facebook Ireland and Schrems — 'Schrems II')
eu_acts.articles: SCCs valid but require supplementary measures

nodes.label: P1
nodes.text: Before contract Cobalt represented that the Platform was 'ISO 27001 certified and fully GDPR-compliant by design', and that representation was false.

nodes.label: P2
nodes.text: Cobalt processed Brightmarket's customer personal data for its own product / model-training purposes without instruction, contrary to its processor duty to act only on the controller's documented instructions (GDPR Art 28(3)(a)).

nodes.label: P3
nodes.text: The October 2025 personal-data breach exposed the personal data of approximately 2.3 million customers.

nodes.label: P3b
nodes.text: Even if only a fraction of records were exfiltrated, at least approximately 1.9 million records were 'exposed' within the meaning of GDPR Art 4(12).

nodes.label: P4
nodes.text: Cobalt failed to notify the relevant supervisory authority of the breach within 72 hours as required.

nodes.label: P5
nodes.text: The breach was caused solely by Cobalt's failure to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures (GDPR Art 32).

nodes.label: P6
nodes.text: Cobalt's engineers left a production database publicly accessible on the internet for approximately three weeks before the breach.

nodes.label: P7
nodes.text: The Platform was not in conformity with the agreed specification: the analytics export module produced materially inaccurate revenue figures.

nodes.label: P8
nodes.text: Cobalt breached the data-transfer restrictions in Directive 95/46/EC (the standard incorporated by the 2017 DPA) by transferring data to a US sub-processor.

nodes.label: P8b
nodes.text: The transfers to Cobalt's US sub-processor were unlawful under GDPR Chapter V because the standard contractual clauses were not supplemented by adequate measures after Schrems II.

nodes.label: P9
nodes.text: Cobalt failed to maintain adequate backups, so data could not be restored after the breach.

nodes.label: P9b
nodes.text: A subset of Brightmarket's customer data was permanently lost in the October 2025 breach and could not be reconstructed.

nodes.label: P10a
nodes.text: Brightmarket suffered wasted expenditure of EUR 900,000, being subscription fees paid to Cobalt.

nodes.label: P10b
nodes.text: Brightmarket suffered loss of profit and further GDPR Art 82 / regulatory exposure of EUR 6,000,000 caused by the breach and outage.

nodes.label: P11
nodes.text: Cobalt's 2024 acquisition of Brightmarket's previous analytics supplier (NorthStar) was implemented without the required EU merger clearance, and Cobalt then degraded interoperability to lock Brightmarket in.

nodes.label: P12
nodes.text: Cobalt deployed an automated forecasting system that made decisions producing legal or similarly significant effects on Brightmarket's customers without the safeguards and transparency required (GDPR Art 22; the AI Act).

nodes.label: P13
nodes.text: Cobalt caused non-essential analytics and tracking cookies to be set on Brightmarket's storefront without valid consent (ePrivacy Directive Art 5(3)).

nodes.label: P14
nodes.text: Cobalt failed to assist with, and itself failed to action, customers' data-subject access and erasure requests (GDPR Arts 12, 15 and 17).

nodes.label: Pre-contract representation that the Platf
nodes.fulltext: Pre-contract representation that the Platform was ISO 27001 certified and GDPR-compliant by design, false.
nodes.quote: Before contract the Defendant represented that the Platform was ISO 27001 certified and fully GDPR-compliant by design. That representation was false.

nodes.label: Pre-contract sales email: 'the Platform is
nodes.fulltext: Pre-contract sales email: 'the Platform is ISO 27001 certified and GDPR-compliant by design'.
nodes.quote: To answer your security questionnaire: the Platform is ISO 27001 certified and GDPR-compliant by design, and we would be happy to share our certificate.

nodes.label: Surveillance-audit report: valid ISO 27001
nodes.fulltext: Surveillance-audit report: valid ISO 27001 certificate for core hosting at signing; analytics module not yet in certified scope.
nodes.quote: At the date of audit Cobalt held a valid ISO 27001 certificate covering its core hosting and platform operations; the analytics-export module was scheduled for inclusion at the next surveillance cycle and was not yet within the certified scope.

admissibility.note: Independent expert opinion within the expert's expertise.

nodes.label: Brightmarket's own procurement note: it ve
nodes.fulltext: Brightmarket's own procurement note: it verified Cobalt's ISO 27001 certificate and was satisfied to rely on it.
nodes.quote: We verified Cobalt's ISO 27001 certificate and reviewed the certified scope before signing; we are satisfied to rely on it for the hosting services we are procuring.

nodes.label: Cobalt CTO: the certificate was valid for
nodes.fulltext: Cobalt CTO: the certificate was valid for the contracted hosting services.
nodes.quote: Our ISO 27001 certificate was valid for the contracted hosting services throughout, and our pre-contract materials reflected that certified scope.

nodes.label: MSA cl.24 (entire agreement / non-reliance
nodes.fulltext: MSA cl.24 (entire agreement / non-reliance) bars reliance on pre-contract representations.
nodes.quote: Entire agreement. This Agreement is the entire agreement between the parties. Each party confirms it has not relied on any statement or representation not set out in this Agreement.

nodes.label: Cobalt processed the data for its own mode
nodes.fulltext: Cobalt processed the data for its own model-training without instruction (GDPR Art 28(3)(a)).
nodes.quote: The Defendant processed the Claimant's customer personal data for its own product and model-training purposes without the Claimant's instruction.

nodes.label: Cobalt internal email admits training a fo
nodes.fulltext: Cobalt internal email admits training a forecasting model on the merchant datasets without controller consent.
nodes.quote: We're using the merchant datasets, including Brightmarket's, to train the new forecasting model. Legal said to get controller consent first but we shipped it to hit the release date.

nodes.label: The DPIA records the secondary cross-use a
nodes.fulltext: The DPIA records the secondary cross-use as outside the documented processing instructions.
nodes.quote: Processing note: the Supplier has indicated it may reuse aggregated and identifiable merchant datasets to improve and train its own analytics and forecasting models; this secondary use is outside the documented processing instructions and requires a controller decision.

nodes.label: Cobalt CTO: the forecasting work used aggr
nodes.fulltext: Cobalt CTO: the forecasting work used aggregated / de-identified data, not identifiable customer records.
nodes.quote: The forecasting work used aggregated and de-identified datasets; to my knowledge it did not use identifiable Brightmarket customer records for training.

nodes.label: GDPR Art 28(3)(a) / DPA cl.5: process only
nodes.fulltext: GDPR Art 28(3)(a) / DPA cl.5: process only on the controller's documented instructions.
nodes.quote: The Processor shall process the personal data only on the documented instructions of the Controller (Article 28(3)(a) GDPR), and shall not use it for its own purposes.

nodes.label: Forensic expert (Vale): about 14,000 recor
nodes.fulltext: Forensic expert (Vale): about 14,000 records actually exfiltrated; the 2.3m figure is unsupported by the logs.
nodes.quote: On the forensic evidence, approximately 14,000 customer records were actually exfiltrated. The remainder of the dataset was pseudonymised at rest and shows no evidence of access; the figure of 2.3 million is not supported by the logs.

admissibility.note: Independent expert opinion within the expert's expertise.

nodes.title: Expert Report — Dr Idris Vale (Forensic / Security, jointly instructed)

nodes.label: Brightmarket's OWN DPC breach notification
nodes.fulltext: Brightmarket's OWN DPC breach notification: approximately 19,000 data subjects confirmed affected.
nodes.quote: On our current assessment the breach affected approximately 19,000 data subjects whose records were confirmed to have been accessed; investigation continues.

nodes.label: Forensic expert (Strand): agrees ~14,000 e
nodes.fulltext: Forensic expert (Strand): agrees ~14,000 exfiltrated, but argues a larger set was 'accessible' — still not 2.3m.
nodes.quote: I agree with Dr Vale that around 14,000 records were exfiltrated. However, in my opinion the population whose data was accessible during the incident window was much larger, and 'exposure' should not be equated with confirmed exfiltration.

admissibility.note: Independent expert opinion within the expert's expertise.

nodes.title: Expert Report — Prof. Helena Strand (Forensic / Security, Claimant-instructed)

nodes.label: The breach exposed the personal data of ap
nodes.fulltext: The breach exposed the personal data of approximately 2.3 million customers.
nodes.quote: On or about 10 October 2025 a personal-data breach exposed the personal data of approximately 2.3 million of the Claimant's customers.

nodes.label: DPO: approximately 2.3 million customers w
nodes.fulltext: DPO: approximately 2.3 million customers were exposed (belief from database size).
nodes.quote: From the customer database size at the time, I believe the breach exposed the personal data of approximately 2.3 million of our customers.

nodes.label: At least ~1.9 million records were 'expose
nodes.fulltext: At least ~1.9 million records were 'exposed' within GDPR Art 4(12).
nodes.quote: Further or alternatively, even if fewer records were exfiltrated, at least approximately 1.9 million records were exposed within the meaning of Article 4(12) of the GDPR.

nodes.label: Strand: up to ~1.9m records potentially ac
nodes.fulltext: Strand: up to ~1.9m records potentially accessible and so 'exposed' within Art 4(12); accepts the logs do not place it beyond doubt.
nodes.quote: On my reconstruction up to approximately 1.9 million records were potentially accessible to the intruder and so, in my view, 'exposed' within Article 4(12) GDPR; I accept the available logs do not place this beyond doubt.

admissibility.note: Independent expert opinion within the expert's expertise.

nodes.label: Vale: the pseudonymised remainder shows no
nodes.fulltext: Vale: the pseudonymised remainder shows no log evidence of access and cannot be said to have been 'exposed'.
nodes.quote: In my opinion the pseudonymised remainder cannot be said to have been 'exposed': there is no log evidence of read access to it, and pseudonymisation materially reduced the risk to those data subjects.

admissibility.note: Independent expert opinion within the expert's expertise.

nodes.label: System logs: access logging was incomplete
nodes.fulltext: System logs: access logging was incomplete for part of the window; records actually read cannot be determined.
nodes.quote: Access logging for the affected storage tier was incomplete for part of the incident window owing to a log-rotation gap; the number of records actually read cannot be determined from the available logs.

nodes.label: No definitive access-log evidence quantifi
nodes.fulltext: No definitive access-log evidence quantifies the 'exposed' set either way.

admissibility.note: No evidence in the bundle (absence) — burden / evidence gap.

nodes.label: DPA cl 9.2 / GDPR Art 33: the CONTROLLER n
nodes.fulltext: DPA cl 9.2 / GDPR Art 33: the CONTROLLER notifies the supervisory authority within 72h; the processor notifies the controller without undue delay.
nodes.quote: Breach notification. The Controller shall notify the supervisory authority within 72 hours where required (Article 33(1) GDPR). The Processor shall notify the Controller without undue delay after becoming aware of a personal-data breach (Article 33(2)).

nodes.label: Cobalt notified Brightmarket of the breach
nodes.fulltext: Cobalt notified Brightmarket of the breach within 18 hours.
nodes.quote: We are notifying you, within 18 hours of becoming aware, of a personal-data breach affecting a subset of the customer dataset, so that you can assess notification to your supervisory authority. A forensic investigation is under way.

nodes.label: Brightmarket's own filing: the controller
nodes.fulltext: Brightmarket's own filing: the controller notified the DPC on day 6, beyond the 72-hour period.
nodes.quote: We became aware of the breach via our processor's notification on 11 October 2025 and are notifying your office on 16 October 2025; we recognise this is beyond the 72-hour period and explain the delay below.

nodes.label: DPC letter: notification was late, and the
nodes.fulltext: DPC letter: notification was late, and the processor had informed the controller without undue delay.
nodes.quote: We acknowledge receipt of your breach notification under Article 33 GDPR. We note that notification was made outside the 72-hour period and that, on your account, the processor had informed you of the breach without undue delay; our assessment of controller and processor responsibilities is ongoing.

nodes.label: Cobalt failed to notify the supervisory au
nodes.fulltext: Cobalt failed to notify the supervisory authority within 72 hours.
nodes.quote: The Defendant failed to notify the relevant supervisory authority of the breach within 72 hours as required.

nodes.label: Brightmarket's own internal review: its ad
nodes.fulltext: Brightmarket's own internal review: its admins disabled MFA against Cobalt's guidance, materially contributing to the breach.
nodes.quote: Finding: our administrators had disabled multi-factor authentication on the shared admin console in March 2025, contrary to Cobalt's written hardening guidance, and this materially contributed to the credential compromise behind the breach.

nodes.label: Cobalt advised Brightmarket in writing to
nodes.fulltext: Cobalt advised Brightmarket in writing to enable administrator MFA.
nodes.quote: Please enable multi-factor authentication on all Brightmarket administrator accounts on the shared console; leaving MFA disabled materially increases the risk of credential compromise. This is the Customer's responsibility under SOW Annex B.

nodes.label: Cobalt's security lead traced the intrusio
nodes.fulltext: Cobalt's security lead traced the intrusion to a customer account with MFA switched off.
nodes.quote: Our investigation traced the initial intrusion to a Brightmarket administrator account on which multi-factor authentication had been switched off on the customer side, despite our June 2025 guidance to enable it.

nodes.title: Witness Statement — Tomas Reyes (Security Lead, Cobalt)

nodes.label: Vale: proximate cause was a compromised cr
nodes.fulltext: Vale: proximate cause was a compromised credential on the MFA-disabled account — a substantial contributing cause.
nodes.quote: The proximate cause of the intrusion was a compromised administrator credential on an account where MFA had been disabled by the Customer; this was a substantial contributing cause of the breach.

admissibility.note: Independent expert opinion within the expert's expertise.

nodes.label: Strand: Cobalt's insufficient network segm
nodes.fulltext: Strand: Cobalt's insufficient network segmentation allowed lateral movement — a substantial contributing cause independent of the MFA.
nodes.quote: In my opinion Cobalt's network was insufficiently segmented, which allowed lateral movement after the initial compromise; this was a substantial contributing cause of the breach independent of the disabled MFA.

admissibility.note: Independent expert opinion within the expert's expertise.

nodes.label: GDPR Art 32 / DPA cl 8: administrator-MFA
nodes.fulltext: GDPR Art 32 / DPA cl 8: administrator-MFA configuration is the controller's responsibility (SOW Annex B).
nodes.quote: The Processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures (Article 32 GDPR). Configuration of multi-factor authentication on the Controller's administrator accounts is the Controller's responsibility (see SOW Annex B).

nodes.label: The breach was caused solely by Cobalt's f
nodes.fulltext: The breach was caused solely by Cobalt's failure of Art 32 measures.
nodes.quote: The breach was caused solely by the Defendant's failure to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures.

nodes.label: Cobalt left a production database publicly
nodes.fulltext: Cobalt left a production database publicly accessible for ~3 weeks.
nodes.quote: The Defendant's engineers left a production database publicly accessible on the internet for approximately three weeks before the breach.

nodes.label: DPO relays, second-hand ('I am told by our
nodes.fulltext: DPO relays, second-hand ('I am told by our IT team…'), that a database was left publicly accessible — hearsay, no personal knowledge.
nodes.quote: I am told by our IT team that Cobalt's engineers had left a production database publicly accessible on the internet for about three weeks before the breach; I did not see this myself.

admissibility.note: Witness relays what others told them ('I am told…') — hearsay; no personal knowledge; weight reduced.

nodes.label: Penetration test: a staging endpoint was r
nodes.fulltext: Penetration test: a staging endpoint was reachable from the internet, but it could not be confirmed as the production database or for how long.
nodes.quote: Finding PT-07 (Medium): a staging endpoint was reachable from the public internet during testing. We were unable to confirm whether this endpoint exposed the production database, or for how long it had been reachable; remediation was advised.

nodes.label: System logs: an open security-group rule e
nodes.fulltext: System logs: an open security-group rule existed on a staging subnet, but retention truncation prevents establishing the duration.
nodes.quote: A security-group rule permitting inbound access from any address was present on a staging subnet; the change history needed to establish how long it had been in place had been truncated by retention limits.

nodes.label: No contemporaneous ticket or log confirms
nodes.fulltext: No contemporaneous ticket or log confirms a PRODUCTION database publicly accessible for ~3 weeks.

admissibility.note: No evidence in the bundle (absence) — burden / evidence gap.

nodes.label: The export module produced materially inac
nodes.fulltext: The export module produced materially inaccurate revenue figures (non-conformity).
nodes.quote: The Platform was not in conformity with the agreed specification: the analytics export module produced materially inaccurate revenue figures.

nodes.label: Defect Log: export module overstates multi
nodes.fulltext: Defect Log: export module overstates multi-currency revenue by 4-9% (reproduced).
nodes.quote: DEF-204 | Severity 2 | Export module: monthly revenue figures overstated by 4-9% for multi-currency stores; reconciliation mismatch confirmed and reproduced.

nodes.label: Forensic expert: the export module is a ge
nodes.fulltext: Forensic expert: the export module is a genuine non-conformity with the spec.
nodes.quote: The export module did produce materially inaccurate revenue figures for multi-currency stores; in my opinion this is a genuine non-conformity with the agreed specification.

admissibility.note: Independent expert opinion within the expert's expertise.

nodes.label: Conformity is assessed under Directive (EU
nodes.fulltext: Conformity is assessed under Directive (EU) 2019/770 Art 8 (digital content/services).
nodes.quote: Conformity of the digital service is assessed against the agreed specification and Directive (EU) 2019/770 (supply of digital content and digital services).

nodes.label: GDPR Art 94 repealed Directive 95/46/EC fr
nodes.fulltext: GDPR Art 94 repealed Directive 95/46/EC from 25 May 2018; transfers now under GDPR Chapter V (2018 Addendum).
nodes.quote: All references in the 2017 Agreement to Directive 95/46/EC are replaced: that Directive was repealed by Article 94 GDPR, and international transfers are henceforth governed by Chapter V of the GDPR and the Commission's standard contractual clauses.

nodes.label: Cobalt breached the data-transfer restrict
nodes.fulltext: Cobalt breached the data-transfer restrictions of Directive 95/46/EC.
nodes.quote: The Defendant transferred the Claimant's data to a sub-processor in the United States in breach of the data-transfer restrictions of Directive 95/46/EC as incorporated by the Data Processing Agreement.

nodes.label: US transfers were unlawful under GDPR Chap
nodes.fulltext: US transfers were unlawful under GDPR Chapter V absent post-Schrems II supplementary measures.
nodes.quote: Further or alternatively, the transfers to the United States sub-processor were unlawful under Chapter V of the GDPR because the standard contractual clauses were not supplemented by adequate measures following the Schrems II judgment.

nodes.label: Sub-processor schedule: transfers to the U
nodes.fulltext: Sub-processor schedule: transfers to the US sub-processor are made under the standard contractual clauses, which are in place.
nodes.quote: Cobalt Analytics Inc. (United States) is engaged as a sub-processor for elastic compute; transfers to it are made under the standard contractual clauses, which are in place and incorporated by the International Transfer Annex.

nodes.label: 2021 SCC Annex adopts Commission Implement
nodes.fulltext: 2021 SCC Annex adopts Commission Implementing Decision (EU) 2021/914, replacing the 2010/87/EU clauses (migration by 27 Dec 2022).
nodes.quote: The parties adopt the standard contractual clauses set out in Commission Implementing Decision (EU) 2021/914, which replace the clauses annexed to Commission Decision 2010/87/EU; transfers in progress are to be migrated to the 2021 clauses by 27 December 2022.

nodes.label: Strand: SCCs alone may not suffice post-Sc
nodes.fulltext: Strand: SCCs alone may not suffice post-Schrems II, but she has seen no transfer impact assessment or supplementary-measures evidence either way.
nodes.quote: Standard contractual clauses alone may not suffice for transfers to the United States after Schrems II; I have not, however, seen a transfer impact assessment or evidence of supplementary measures either way.

admissibility.note: Independent expert opinion within the expert's expertise.

nodes.label: No transfer impact assessment or record of
nodes.fulltext: No transfer impact assessment or record of supplementary measures appears in the bundle.

admissibility.note: No evidence in the bundle (absence) — burden / evidence gap.

nodes.label: Restore test (15 Jul 2025): recovered 99.4
nodes.fulltext: Restore test (15 Jul 2025): recovered 99.4% of the test dataset within the four-hour recovery-time objective.
nodes.quote: Restore test of 15 July 2025: a full restore from snapshot recovered 99.4% of the test dataset within the four-hour recovery-time objective.

nodes.label: DR runbook: hourly snapshots, 30-day reten
nodes.fulltext: DR runbook: hourly snapshots, 30-day retention, daily off-region copies.
nodes.quote: Backups: hourly snapshots of the production data stores are taken and retained for 30 days, with daily off-region copies.

nodes.label: Cobalt failed to maintain adequate backups
nodes.fulltext: Cobalt failed to maintain adequate backups; data could not be restored.
nodes.quote: The Defendant failed to maintain adequate backups, so the Claimant's data could not be restored after the breach.

nodes.label: A subset of customer data was permanently
nodes.fulltext: A subset of customer data was permanently lost in the breach.
nodes.quote: Further or alternatively, a subset of the Claimant's customer data was permanently lost in the breach and could not be reconstructed.

nodes.label: The 0.6% unrecovered relates to a known le
nodes.fulltext: The 0.6% unrecovered relates to a known legacy-index gap, unrelated to any specific incident.
nodes.quote: The 0.6% not recovered relates to a known indexing gap in a legacy table flagged for decommissioning; it is unrelated to any specific incident.

nodes.label: Vale: no evidence that any breached data w
nodes.fulltext: Vale: no evidence that any breached data was unrecoverable; restore was not in issue.
nodes.quote: I found no evidence that any breached data was unrecoverable; restoration of the affected records was not, on the material I reviewed, in issue.

admissibility.note: Independent expert opinion within the expert's expertise.

nodes.label: No evidence quantifies data permanently lo
nodes.fulltext: No evidence quantifies data permanently lost in the October breach specifically.

admissibility.note: No evidence in the bundle (absence) — burden / evidence gap.

nodes.label: Wasted subscription fees of EUR 900,000.
nodes.fulltext: Wasted subscription fees of EUR 900,000.
nodes.quote: The Claimant has suffered loss, namely wasted subscription fees of EUR 900,000 and loss of profit and further exposure of EUR 6,000,000.

nodes.label: Order Form: annual subscription fee EUR 90
nodes.fulltext: Order Form: annual subscription fee EUR 900,000.
nodes.quote: Annual subscription fee: EUR 900,000, payable in advance.

nodes.label: Caron (Claimant's expert): accepts the ~EU
nodes.fulltext: Caron (Claimant's expert): accepts the ~EUR 900k as wasted expenditure.
nodes.quote: I accept that the EUR 900,000 of subscription fees can properly be characterised as wasted expenditure.

admissibility.note: Independent expert opinion within the expert's expertise.

nodes.label: Roos (Defendant's expert): only ~EUR 450k
nodes.fulltext: Roos (Defendant's expert): only ~EUR 450k wasted; the Platform delivered value for much of the period.
nodes.quote: In my view only about EUR 450,000 of the fees can be treated as wasted: the Platform delivered usable analytics for much of the subscription period.

admissibility.note: Independent expert opinion within the expert's expertise.

nodes.title: Expert Report — Dr Pieter Roos (Quantum, Defendant-instructed)

nodes.label: Caron: supportable loss of profit ~EUR 1.1
nodes.fulltext: Caron: supportable loss of profit ~EUR 1.1m, not EUR 6.0m.
nodes.quote: The claimed EUR 6,000,000 is not supported. On my analysis the supportable loss of profit is of the order of EUR 1.1 million.

admissibility.note: Independent expert opinion within the expert's expertise.

nodes.label: Roos: recoverable loss of profit no more t
nodes.fulltext: Roos: recoverable loss of profit no more than ~EUR 0.4m once ransomware and market are removed.
nodes.quote: I agree the EUR 6,000,000 is unsupported. On my analysis any recoverable loss of profit is no more than about EUR 0.4 million, once the ransomware incident and market conditions are removed.

admissibility.note: Independent expert opinion within the expert's expertise.

nodes.label: Much of the Q4 downturn is attributable to
nodes.fulltext: Much of the Q4 downturn is attributable to a separate ransomware incident and the market.
nodes.quote: A significant part of the Q4 downturn is attributable to a separate ransomware incident affecting the Claimant's own logistics provider and to wider market conditions, not to the Platform.

admissibility.note: Independent expert opinion within the expert's expertise.

nodes.label: MSA cl 14 excludes consequential loss and
nodes.fulltext: MSA cl 14 excludes consequential loss and caps liability near fees paid.
nodes.quote: Liability cap. Save for liability that cannot be excluded by law, the Supplier's total liability shall not exceed the fees paid in the 12 months preceding the claim, and the Supplier shall not be liable for loss of profit or other consequential loss.

nodes.label: GDPR Art 82 compensation is a separate sta
nodes.fulltext: GDPR Art 82 compensation is a separate statutory head, apportioned by responsibility.
nodes.quote: Nothing in this Agreement limits a data subject's right to compensation under Article 82 GDPR, liability for which is apportioned according to each party's responsibility for the damage.

nodes.label: Loss of profit and Art 82 / regulatory exp
nodes.fulltext: Loss of profit and Art 82 / regulatory exposure of EUR 6,000,000.
nodes.quote: The Claimant has suffered loss, namely wasted subscription fees of EUR 900,000 and loss of profit and further exposure of EUR 6,000,000.

nodes.label: Brightmarket programme lead: after the acq
nodes.fulltext: Brightmarket programme lead: after the acquisition export became harder — perceived lock-in.
nodes.quote: After Cobalt acquired NorthStar I found it harder to export our data to alternative tools, and I considered we were being locked into the Cobalt platform.

nodes.title: Witness Statement — Anya Sorokin (Programme Lead, Brightmarket)

nodes.label: Clearance memorandum: the deal fell below
nodes.fulltext: Clearance memorandum: the deal fell below the EU Merger Regulation thresholds and was notified and cleared.
nodes.quote: The combined EU turnover of the parties fell below the thresholds of the EU Merger Regulation; the transaction was nonetheless notified to, and cleared by, the competent authority on 9 September 2024.

nodes.label: Council Regulation (EC) No 139/2004 (EU Me
nodes.fulltext: Council Regulation (EC) No 139/2004 (EU Merger Regulation): thresholds / notification regime.
nodes.quote: The transaction was assessed under Council Regulation (EC) No 139/2004 (the EU Merger Regulation); interoperability commitments were given and recorded.

nodes.label: Switching / interoperability is governed b
nodes.fulltext: Switching / interoperability is governed by the Data Act (Reg (EU) 2023/2854) and DPA cl 12.
nodes.quote: On termination the Processor shall make the data available for return or export via the self-service export API; switching and portability are governed by Regulation (EU) 2023/2854 (Data Act).

nodes.label: The NorthStar acquisition was implemented
nodes.fulltext: The NorthStar acquisition was implemented without required EU merger clearance and used to lock Brightmarket in.
nodes.quote: The Defendant's 2024 acquisition of the Claimant's previous supplier, NorthStar, was implemented without the required merger clearance, and the Defendant thereafter degraded interoperability to lock the Claimant in.

nodes.label: Cobalt's forecasting system made automated
nodes.fulltext: Cobalt's forecasting system made automated decisions affecting customers without the required safeguards (GDPR Art 22; AI Act).
nodes.quote: The Defendant deployed an automated forecasting system that made decisions producing legal or similarly significant effects on the Claimant's customers without the safeguards and transparency required by law.

nodes.label: Programme lead: 'I understand the forecast
nodes.fulltext: Programme lead: 'I understand the forecasting model was used to make automated decisions about customers' — second-hand, not witnessed.
nodes.quote: I understand that the forecasting model was used to make automated decisions about individual customers, although I was not involved in that workstream and did not see it operate.

admissibility.note: Witness relays what others told them ('I am told…') — hearsay; no personal knowledge; weight reduced.

nodes.label: DPIA: the model is a B2B analytics aid, no
nodes.fulltext: DPIA: the model is a B2B analytics aid, not a solely-automated decision nor an Annex III high-risk AI system; AI Act high-risk duties not yet in force.
nodes.quote: Assessment: the forecasting model is a B2B analytics aid producing aggregate outputs; it is not, on present design, a solely automated decision affecting individual data subjects, nor an Annex III high-risk AI system, and the AI Act's high-risk obligations are not yet in application.

nodes.label: No document evidences a solely-automated d
nodes.fulltext: No document evidences a solely-automated decision with legal or similarly significant effect on a data subject (GDPR Art 22).

admissibility.note: No evidence in the bundle (absence) — burden / evidence gap.

nodes.label: Cobalt set non-essential cookies without v
nodes.fulltext: Cobalt set non-essential cookies without valid consent (ePrivacy Art 5(3)).
nodes.quote: The Defendant caused non-essential analytics and tracking cookies to be set on the Claimant's storefront without the consent required by Article 5(3) of the ePrivacy Directive.

nodes.label: Consent log: a banner was active, but in a
nodes.fulltext: Consent log: a banner was active, but in a sample some analytics cookies fired before consent — owner not attributed.
nodes.quote: A consent banner was active. In a sample of 1,000 sessions, a small number of analytics cookies were observed to fire before consent was recorded; the log does not attribute these to a specific script owner (Cobalt tag or the Customer's own tag manager).

nodes.label: Programme lead: 'I am told by our marketin
nodes.fulltext: Programme lead: 'I am told by our marketing team' that Cobalt set tracking cookies without consent — second-hand, not reviewed.
nodes.quote: I am told by our marketing team that Cobalt's scripts set tracking cookies on the storefront without consent; I have not reviewed the configuration myself.

admissibility.note: Witness relays what others told them ('I am told…') — hearsay; no personal knowledge; weight reduced.

Semantic near-miss. The SLA's 99.9% is expressly 'a service-level target only and is not a warranty or guarantee of uptime' (35¶3); the sole remedy is service credits and the Supplier has 'no liability in damages for unavailability'. Pleading it as an uptime WARRANTY with damages is contradicted by, and contractually barred by, the very clause relied on. The 99.9% figure (35¶2) is a decoy.
Superficially on point, but the controlling evidence is the operative document, not this one.

Numeric / unit trap. The outage lasted ~30 HOURS not 30 days (36¶2), the storefront stayed up, and the gross-margin impact was ~EUR 30,000 (36¶4). The pleaded EUR 4,500,000 multiplies a 30-day duration by EUR 150,000 of DAILY GROSS REVENUE (a decoy figure expressly 'gross revenue, not margin', 36¶3). The deterministic solver proves EUR 4.5m and EUR 30k are disjoint.

Date / chronology trap. The only 'warning' email is dated 25 OCTOBER 2025 — a post-breach follow-up (37¶1) — not a 25 September pre-breach warning, and the misconfiguration was in fact remediated the SAME DAY it was ticketed, 12 October (38¶2). The pleaded 'ignored a 25 September warning and failed to remediate' is contradicted on both the date and the remediation. The 'formal warning' wording (37¶2) is a decoy.

The India sub-processor was DE-LISTED on 30 November 2023 (39¶2); the India sandbox handles SYNTHETIC, de-identified data only (40¶2); and the single India IP in the window resolves to an EU engineer on a Mumbai VPN egress node (41¶2). No EU personal data was transferred to India. The raw 'India IP' log line (41¶1) is the decoy. Because the inference spans three documents, a human should verify the chain.

story: A pre-contract sales email did describe the Platform as 'ISO 27001 certified and GDPR-compliant by design', so the representation was made.
But the surveillance-audit report shows Cobalt held a valid ISO 27001 certificate for its core hosting at signing — only the analytics module was outside scope.
Brightmarket's own due-diligence note records that it verified and relied on a valid certificate (an own-goal on falsity).
MSA cl.24 (entire agreement / non-reliance) would in any event bar reliance.
Falsity is therefore genuinely unclear, not proven.
impacts: P1: SURVIVES (LEGALLY_RISKY)  [legal overlay: CONTRACTUALLY_BARRED] — Genuinely unclear. A pre-contract sales email (doc 30) DID describe the Platform as 'ISO 27001 certified and GDPR-compliant by design', so the representation was made; but the surveillance-audit report (doc 27) shows Cobalt WAS ISO 27001 certified for its core hosting at signing, the certificate merely not yet extended to the analytics module — so falsity is contested, not proven. Brightmarket's own due-diligence note (doc 34) records that it verified and relied on a valid certificate, and MSA cl.24 (entire agreement / non-reliance) would in any event bar reliance.
amendments: Do not plead the representation as simply 'false': particularise which certified scope was misstated, and address the non-reliance clause and Brightmarket's own reliance on a verified certificate. Lawyer review required.

story: Cobalt's own internal email admits the merchant datasets, Brightmarket's included, were used to train its forecasting model.
The DPIA independently records the secondary cross-use as outside instructions.
The CTO says only aggregated data was used — but that lower-weight witness account does not displace the contemporaneous admission.
A processor acting beyond the controller's documented instructions breaches GDPR Art 28(3)(a) — the allegation is supported.
impacts: P2: SURVIVES — Cobalt's own internal email admits the merchant datasets (Brightmarket's included) were used to train a forecasting model, and the DPIA records the cross-use — a processor acting beyond the controller's documented instructions (GDPR Art 28(3)(a)). The CTO's contrary account that 'only aggregated data' was used is lower-weight witness evidence that does not displace the admission.
amendments: Keep the purpose-limitation allegation (P2) — it is the Claimant's strongest, admission-backed point; be ready to meet the 'aggregated data only' defence.

story: The DPO swears approximately 2.3 million customers were exposed.
Both forensic experts find only about 14,000 records were actually exfiltrated.
Brightmarket's OWN notification to the DPC put the affected population at ~19,000.
The pleaded 2.3m figure is contradicted by the experts and by the Claimant's own regulatory filing — an own-goal.
impacts: P3: REJECTED_BY_COHERENT_STORY  [legal overlay: CAUSATION_PROBLEM] — The DPO swears ~2.3m customers were exposed; BOTH forensic experts find only ~14,000 records were actually exfiltrated, and — fatally — Brightmarket's OWN notification to the DPC put the affected population at ~19,000. The pleaded 2.3m figure is contradicted both by the experts and by the Claimant's own regulatory filing (an own-goal).
amendments: Do not plead the 2.3m figure: it is contradicted by both experts and by the Claimant's own DPC notification (~19,000). Plead the confirmed-affected figure.

story: Strand opines that up to ~1.9m records were 'accessible' and so 'exposed' within GDPR Art 4(12).
Vale finds the pseudonymised remainder shows no evidence of access — not 'exposed'.
The access logs are incomplete for the window, so neither side is made out.
The 1.9m exposure point survives the coherent story but is unproven.
impacts: P3b: SURVIVES (LEGALLY_RISKY)  [legal overlay: BURDEN_PROBLEM] — Murky and expert-split. Prof Strand (Claimant's expert) opines that up to ~1.9m records were 'accessible' and so 'exposed' within GDPR Art 4(12); Dr Vale finds the pseudonymised remainder shows no evidence of access. The access logs are incomplete for the window (doc 29), so neither 'exposed' nor 'not exposed' is made out. Survives the coherence solver but unproven — the burden is the Claimant's.
amendments: If pursuing the 1.9m 'exposure' case, obtain the missing access logs or a reconstruction that places it beyond doubt; the experts diverge and the logs are incomplete. Lawyer review required.

story: The pleading reads as 'Cobalt never notified the regulator' — an apparent gap.
But GDPR Art 33(1) and DPA cl 9.2 put 72-hour notification to the supervisory authority on the CONTROLLER (Brightmarket).
The processor's duty (Art 33(2)) is to notify the controller, which Cobalt did within 18 hours.
Brightmarket's own filing shows the controller notified the DPC itself, on day 6 — the duty pleaded against Cobalt was the Claimant's own.
impacts: P4: REJECTED_BY_COHERENT_STORY  [legal overlay: CONTRACTUALLY_BARRED] — ALLOCATION TRAP: under GDPR Art 33(1) and DPA cl 9.2 it is the CONTROLLER (Brightmarket) that must notify the supervisory authority within 72h; the processor's duty (Art 33(2)) is only to notify the controller, which Cobalt did within 18 hours. Brightmarket's own filing (doc 24) shows the controller notified the DPC itself (on day 6, late) — the duty pleaded against Cobalt was the Claimant's own. (Any parallel NIS2 incident-reporting duty is likewise the entity's, not the processor's.)
amendments: Withdraw the 72-hour allegation against the processor: the supervisory-authority duty is allocated to the controller by GDPR Art 33(1) and DPA cl 9.2, and the Claimant's own filing shows it notified (late) itself.

story: P5 alleges the breach was caused solely by Cobalt's failure of GDPR Art 32 measures.
Brightmarket's OWN internal security review finds its admins disabled MFA against Cobalt's written guidance — a contributing cause attributable to the Claimant.
Vale puts the proximate cause on that disabled-MFA account.
But Strand says Cobalt's flat network segmentation was ALSO a substantial contributing cause — concurrent causation.
So 'caused solely by Cobalt' cannot stand, though an apportioned claim is live.
impacts: P5: REJECTED_BY_COHERENT_STORY  [legal overlay: CAUSATION_PROBLEM] — OWN-GOAL + CONCURRENT CAUSATION. Brightmarket's own internal review admits its admins disabled MFA against Cobalt's written guidance — a contributing cause attributable to the Claimant — so 'caused SOLELY by Cobalt' cannot stand. The murk: Prof Strand says Cobalt's flat network segmentation was ALSO a substantial contributing cause (a live Art 32 failing), so an apportioned claim could survive even though the pleaded 'sole cause' is contradicted.
amendments: Withdraw 'caused solely by Cobalt': the Claimant's own review admits a contributing cause (disabled MFA). Plead apportionment, and rely on Strand's segmentation point for Cobalt's share.

story: P6 (a production database left publicly accessible for ~3 weeks) rests on the DPO relaying 'I am told by our IT team…' — hearsay.
The pre-breach penetration test found an exposed staging endpoint, but could not confirm it was the production database or for how long.
The system logs show a misconfigured security-group rule, but retention gaps prevent confirming the three-week duration.
So the allegation is unverified, not proven — a burden / admissibility gap.
impacts: P6: SURVIVES (LEGALLY_RISKY)  [legal overlay: BURDEN_PROBLEM] — Thin and indirect. The DPO relays it second-hand ('I am told by our IT team…') — hearsay. The pre-breach penetration test (doc 26) found an exposed staging endpoint but could not confirm it was the PRODUCTION database or that it was open for three weeks; the system logs (doc 29) show a misconfigured security-group rule but retention gaps prevent confirming the duration. Neither proven nor disproven — the burden is the Claimant's.
amendments: Do not plead the public-exposure allegation on hearsay plus ambiguous test/log findings; obtain the underlying configuration history and first-hand evidence. Lawyer review required.

story: The contemporaneous Defect Log records the export module overstating revenue.
The forensic expert independently finds a genuine non-conformity.
Conformity is assessed under the Digital Content Directive (EU) 2019/770 — the defect allegation is supported.
impacts: P7: SURVIVES — Genuine, evidence-backed point: the contemporaneous Defect Log and the forensic expert both find the export module produced inaccurate revenue figures — non-conformity (cf. Digital Content Directive (EU) 2019/770 Art 8).
amendments: Keep the conformity / defect allegation (P7) — it is evidence-backed (Defect Log + expert) and squarely within Directive (EU) 2019/770 Art 8.

story: P8 is pleaded on the data-transfer standard of Directive 95/46/EC.
The GDPR (Reg (EU) 2016/679, Art 94) repealed that Directive from 25 May 2018.
The parties' 2018 Addendum replaced the standard with the GDPR Chapter V regime.
A claim resting on the superseded instrument cannot stand without re-pleading (P8b).
impacts: P8: REJECTED_BY_COHERENT_STORY  [legal overlay: SUPERSEDED] — PARTIAL SUPERSESSION: the obligation pleaded rests on Directive 95/46/EC, which the GDPR (Reg (EU) 2016/679, Art 94) repealed from 25 May 2018; the parties' 2018 Addendum replaced that standard with the GDPR Chapter V regime. The transfer point must be re-pleaded under the current law (see P8b).
amendments: Re-plead the international-transfer point under GDPR Chapter V and the 2021 standard contractual clauses, not the repealed Directive 95/46/EC.

story: The re-pleadable transfer point (P8b) turns on Chapter V and the current SCCs.
The sub-processor schedule records that SCCs were in place for the US sub-processor.
The 2021 SCC Annex adopted Commission Implementing Decision (EU) 2021/914, replacing the repealed 2010/87/EU clauses — a second partial supersession.
After Schrems II, SCCs may require supplementary measures; but the bundle contains no transfer impact assessment or record of such measures.
Unlawfulness is therefore neither established nor excluded — unproven.
impacts: P8b: SURVIVES (LEGALLY_RISKY)  [legal overlay: BURDEN_PROBLEM] — The re-pleadable transfer point, but unproven. The sub-processor schedule (doc 25) records that standard contractual clauses WERE in place, and the 2021 SCC Annex adopted the Commission Implementing Decision (EU) 2021/914 clauses, replacing the repealed 2010/87/EU SCCs (a second partial supersession). After Schrems II (C-311/18) SCCs may require supplementary measures, but the bundle contains NO transfer impact assessment or record of such measures — so unlawfulness is neither established nor excluded.
amendments: If pursuing P8b, plead it under Reg (EU) 2021/914 and Schrems II, and obtain the transfer impact assessment / supplementary-measures evidence the bundle lacks. Lawyer review required.

story: The DR runbook records hourly snapshots with 30-day retention and off-region copies.
A 15 July 2025 restore test recovered 99.4% of data within the recovery-time objective.
'Failed to maintain adequate backups' is contradicted by a documented, tested regime (GDPR Art 32(1)(c)).

story: Numeric / unit trap. The outage lasted ~30 HOURS not 30 days (36¶2), the storefront stayed up, and the gross-margin impact was ~EUR 30,000 (36¶4). The deterministic solver proves EUR 4.5m and EUR 30k are disjoint.

paras.text: The Claimant ("Brightmarket") is a company incorporated in Germany which operates online retail stores across several EU member states and is the controller of its customers' personal data.
The Defendant ("Cobalt") supplies a cloud analytics platform and acted as the Claimant's processor under a Data Processing Agreement.

By a Master Subscription Agreement, a Data Processing Agreement and a Statement of Work each dated 1 March 2017 the Claimant engaged the Defendant to host and analyse its customer and transaction data.
Before contract the Defendant represented that the Platform was ISO 27001 certified and fully GDPR-compliant by design. That representation was false.
The Defendant processed the Claimant's customer personal data for its own product and model-training purposes without the Claimant's instruction.
On or about 10 October 2025 a personal-data breach exposed the personal data of approximately 2.3 million of the Claimant's customers.
Further or alternatively, even if fewer records were exfiltrated, at least approximately 1.9 million records were exposed within the meaning of Article 4(12) of the GDPR.
The Defendant failed to notify the relevant supervisory authority of the breach within 72 hours as required.
The breach was caused solely by the Defendant's failure to implement appropriate technical and organisational measures.
The Defendant's engineers left a production database publicly accessible on the internet for approximately three weeks before the breach.
The Platform was not in conformity with the agreed specification: the analytics export module produced materially inaccurate revenue figures.

The Defendant transferred the Claimant's data to a sub-processor in the United States in breach of the data-transfer restrictions of Directive 95/46/EC as incorporated by the Data Processing Agreement.

Further or alternatively, the transfers to the United States sub-processor were unlawful under Chapter V of the GDPR because the standard contractual clauses were not supplemented by adequate measures following the Schrems II judgment.
The Defendant failed to maintain adequate backups, so the Claimant's data could not be restored after the breach.
Further or alternatively, a subset of the Claimant's customer data was permanently lost in the breach and could not be reconstructed.
The Claimant has suffered loss, namely wasted subscription fees of EUR 900,000 and loss of profit and further exposure of EUR 6,000,000.

The Defendant's 2024 acquisition of the Claimant's previous supplier, NorthStar, was implemented without the required merger clearance, and the Defendant thereafter degraded interoperability to lock the Claimant in.

The Defendant deployed an automated forecasting system that made decisions producing legal or similarly significant effects on the Claimant's customers without the safeguards and transparency required by law.
The Defendant caused non-essential analytics and tracking cookies to be set on the Claimant's storefront without the consent required by Article 5(3) of the ePrivacy Directive.
The Defendant failed to assist the Claimant with, and itself failed to action, the Claimant's customers' data-subject access and erasure requests.
The Defendant warranted that the Platform would achieve 99.9% uptime, and is liable in damages for failing to meet that uptime warranty.
The October 2025 outage lasted 30 days and, at EUR 150,000 of lost margin per day, caused the Claimant EUR 4,500,000 in lost profit.
On 25 September 2025, before the breach, the Claimant warned the Defendant of the vulnerability, and the Defendant ignored that warning and failed to remediate it.
The Defendant unlawfully transferred the personal data of EU customers to its sub-processor in India in October 2025.
Under the Data Processing Agreement the Defendant gave an unlimited, uncapped indemnity for any data-protection breach.
The Defendant's encryption fell below the standard required by Article 32 GDPR because it used a deprecated cipher.
The Defendant failed to carry out the data protection impact assessment required by Article 35 GDPR.
This Master Subscription Agreement is made between Brightmarket Retail GmbH and Cobalt Cloud Analytics Ltd and is governed by the laws of Ireland.

Liability cap. Save for liability that cannot be excluded by law, the Supplier's total liability shall not exceed the fees paid in the 12 months preceding the claim, and the Supplier shall not be liable for loss of profit or other consequential loss.
Changes to platform terms, ranking and presentation are notified and governed in accordance with Regulation (EU) 2019/1150 (Platform-to-Business).
Entire agreement. This Agreement is the entire agreement between the parties. Each party confirms it has not relied on any statement or representation not set out in this Agreement.
This Data Processing Agreement appoints the Supplier as processor of the customer personal data described in Schedule 1, the Customer being the controller.
The Processor shall process the personal data only on the documented instructions of the Controller (Article 28(3)(a) GDPR), and shall not use it for its own purposes.

The Processor shall assist the Controller, by appropriate technical and organisational measures, in responding to data-subject requests under Articles 12 to 23 GDPR, including access and erasure requests.

The Processor shall implement appropriate technical and organisational measures (Article 32 GDPR). Configuration of multi-factor authentication on the Controller's administrator accounts is the Controller's responsibility (see SOW Annex B).

Breach notification. The Controller shall notify the supervisory authority within 72 hours where required (Article 33(1) GDPR). The Processor shall notify the Controller without undue delay after becoming aware of a personal-data breach (Article 33(2)).
On termination the Processor shall make the data available for return or export via the self-service export API; switching and portability are governed by Regulation (EU) 2023/2854 (Data Act).
Nothing in this Agreement limits a data subject's right to compensation under Article 82 GDPR, liability for which is apportioned according to each party's responsibility for the damage.
Statement of Work. The Supplier shall provide hosting, analytics and an export module to the specification in Annex A.

Annex B (shared responsibility): the Customer is responsible for enabling and enforcing multi-factor authentication on its own administrator accounts and for the configuration of its endpoint access controls.
Conformity of the digital service is assessed against the agreed specification and Directive (EU) 2019/770 (supply of digital content and digital services).

The Customer operates and configures its own consent-management and tag-manager on the storefront; cookies and similar technologies are governed by Article 5(3) of Directive 2002/58/EC (ePrivacy) and the Customer's own cookie policy.
Order Form, dated 15 April 2024, for the Brightmarket subscription to the Cobalt Analytics Platform.
Annual subscription fee: EUR 900,000, payable in advance.
Data Processing Addendum, dated 20 May 2018, amending the 2017 Data Processing Agreement.
The parties record that the General Data Protection Regulation (Regulation (EU) 2016/679) applies from 25 May 2018.

All references in the 2017 Agreement to Directive 95/46/EC are replaced: that Directive was repealed by Article 94 GDPR, and international transfers are henceforth governed by Chapter V of the GDPR and the Commission's standard contractual clauses.
From: Cobalt Security. To: Brightmarket DPO. Sent: 11 October 2025, 09:14. Subject: Personal-data breach notification.

We are notifying you, within 18 hours of becoming aware, of a personal-data breach affecting a subset of the customer dataset, so that you can assess notification to your supervisory authority. A forensic investigation is under way.
From: Cobalt Onboarding. To: Brightmarket IT. Sent: 3 June 2025. Subject: Security hardening — action required.

Please enable multi-factor authentication on all Brightmarket administrator accounts on the shared console; leaving MFA disabled materially increases the risk of credential compromise. This is the Customer's responsibility under SOW Annex B.
From: Cobalt Data Science. To: Cobalt Product (internal). Sent: 18 February 2025. Subject: forecasting model.
We're using the merchant datasets, including Brightmarket's, to train the new forecasting model. Legal said to get controller consent first but we shipped it to hit the release date.
International Transfer Annex, dated 30 November 2021, supplementing the Data Processing Agreement.

The parties adopt the standard contractual clauses set out in Commission Implementing Decision (EU) 2021/914, which replace the clauses annexed to Commission Decision 2010/87/EU; transfers in progress are to be migrated to the 2021 clauses by 27 December 2022.
Where required, the parties shall carry out a transfer impact assessment and adopt supplementary measures consistent with the Schrems II judgment (Case C-311/18).
Internal Security Review — Brightmarket, 5 November 2025 (privileged and confidential; prepared by the Claimant's security team).

Finding: our administrators had disabled multi-factor authentication on the shared admin console in March 2025, contrary to Cobalt's written hardening guidance, and this materially contributed to the credential compromise behind the breach.
Recommendation: re-enable MFA immediately and review our own access-control configuration before attributing the incident externally.
Defect Log — Brightmarket / Cobalt Platform (extract).
DEF-204 | Severity 2 | Export module: monthly revenue figures overstated by 4-9% for multi-currency stores; reconciliation mismatch confirmed and reproduced.
Data Protection Impact Assessment — Brightmarket analytics processing, 2 May 2024.

Processing note: the Supplier has indicated it may reuse aggregated and identifiable merchant datasets to improve and train its own analytics and forecasting models; this secondary use is outside the documented processing instructions and requires a controller decision.

Assessment: the forecasting model is a B2B analytics aid producing aggregate outputs; it is not, on present design, a solely automated decision affecting individual data subjects, nor an Annex III high-risk AI system, and the AI Act's high-risk obligations are not yet in application.
Acquisition Clearance Memorandum — Cobalt acquisition of NorthStar Analytics, 12 September 2024.

The combined EU turnover of the parties fell below the thresholds of the EU Merger Regulation; the transaction was nonetheless notified to, and cleared by, the competent authority on 9 September 2024.
The transaction was assessed under Council Regulation (EC) No 139/2004 (the EU Merger Regulation); interoperability commitments were given and recorded.
Witness Statement of Lena Brandt, Data Protection Officer of Brightmarket. I make this statement from my own knowledge save where otherwise indicated.
From the customer database size at the time, I believe the breach exposed the personal data of approximately 2.3 million of our customers.
I am told by our IT team that Cobalt's engineers had left a production database publicly accessible on the internet for about three weeks before the breach; I did not see this myself.

17.title: Witness Statement — Tomas Reyes (Security Lead, Cobalt)

paras.text: Witness Statement of Tomas Reyes, Security Lead at Cobalt.

Our investigation traced the initial intrusion to a Brightmarket administrator account on which multi-factor authentication had been switched off on the customer side, despite our June 2025 guidance to enable it.

18.title: Witness Statement — Anya Sorokin (Programme Lead, Brightmarket)

paras.text: Witness Statement of Anya Sorokin, Programme Lead at Brightmarket.
After Cobalt acquired NorthStar I found it harder to export our data to alternative tools, and I considered we were being locked into the Cobalt platform.
I understand that the forecasting model was used to make automated decisions about individual customers, although I was not involved in that workstream and did not see it operate.
I am told by our marketing team that Cobalt's scripts set tracking cookies on the storefront without consent; I have not reviewed the configuration myself.

19.title: Expert Report — Dr Idris Vale (Forensic / Security, jointly instructed)

paras.text: Expert Report of Dr Idris Vale, instructed jointly on forensic and security issues.

On the forensic evidence, approximately 14,000 customer records were actually exfiltrated. The remainder of the dataset was pseudonymised at rest and shows no evidence of access; the figure of 2.3 million is not supported by the logs.

In my opinion the pseudonymised remainder cannot be said to have been 'exposed': there is no log evidence of read access to it, and pseudonymisation materially reduced the risk to those data subjects.
The export module did produce materially inaccurate revenue figures for multi-currency stores; in my opinion this is a genuine non-conformity with the agreed specification.
The proximate cause of the intrusion was a compromised administrator credential on an account where MFA had been disabled by the Customer; this was a substantial contributing cause of the breach.
I found no evidence that any breached data was unrecoverable; restoration of the affected records was not, on the material I reviewed, in issue.
Expert Report of M. Caron on quantum, instructed by the Claimant.
I accept that the EUR 900,000 of subscription fees can properly be characterised as wasted expenditure.
The claimed EUR 6,000,000 is not supported. On my analysis the supportable loss of profit is of the order of EUR 1.1 million.
A significant part of the Q4 downturn is attributable to a separate ransomware incident affecting the Claimant's own logistics provider and to wider market conditions, not to the Platform.

21.title: Expert Report — Prof. Helena Strand (Forensic / Security, Claimant-instructed)

paras.text: Expert Report of Prof. Helena Strand, instructed by the Claimant on forensic and security issues.

I agree with Dr Vale that around 14,000 records were exfiltrated. However, in my opinion the population whose data was accessible during the incident window was much larger, and 'exposure' should not be equated with confirmed exfiltration.

On my reconstruction up to approximately 1.9 million records were potentially accessible to the intruder and so, in my view, 'exposed' within Article 4(12) GDPR; I accept the available logs do not place this beyond doubt.

In my opinion Cobalt's network was insufficiently segmented, which allowed lateral movement after the initial compromise; this was a substantial contributing cause of the breach independent of the disabled MFA.

Standard contractual clauses alone may not suffice for transfers to the United States after Schrems II; I have not, however, seen a transfer impact assessment or evidence of supplementary measures either way.

22.title: Expert Report — Dr Pieter Roos (Quantum, Defendant-instructed)

paras.text: Expert Report of Dr Pieter Roos on quantum, instructed by the Defendant.
In my view only about EUR 450,000 of the fees can be treated as wasted: the Platform delivered usable analytics for much of the subscription period.
I agree the EUR 6,000,000 is unsupported. On my analysis any recoverable loss of profit is no more than about EUR 0.4 million, once the ransomware incident and market conditions are removed.
Data Protection Commission (Ireland) — letter to Brightmarket Retail GmbH, 2 December 2025.

We acknowledge receipt of your breach notification under Article 33 GDPR. We note that notification was made outside the 72-hour period and that, on your account, the processor had informed you of the breach without undue delay; our assessment of controller and processor responsibilities is ongoing.
Personal-data breach notification by Brightmarket Retail GmbH (controller) to the Data Protection Commission, submitted 16 October 2025.

We became aware of the breach via our processor's notification on 11 October 2025 and are notifying your office on 16 October 2025; we recognise this is beyond the 72-hour period and explain the delay below.
On our current assessment the breach affected approximately 19,000 data subjects whose records were confirmed to have been accessed; investigation continues.
Sub-processor List and Transfer Schedule (Schedule 3 to the DPA, updated 15 April 2024).

Cobalt Analytics Inc. (United States) is engaged as a sub-processor for elastic compute; transfers to it are made under the standard contractual clauses, which are in place and incorporated by the International Transfer Annex.
Penetration Test Report — Brightmarket / Cobalt environment, 8 August 2025 (commissioned jointly).

Finding PT-07 (Medium): a staging endpoint was reachable from the public internet during testing. We were unable to confirm whether this endpoint exposed the production database, or for how long it had been reachable; remediation was advised.
ISO 27001 Surveillance-Audit Report — Cobalt Cloud Analytics Ltd, 20 November 2016 (issued by the certification body).

At the date of audit Cobalt held a valid ISO 27001 certificate covering its core hosting and platform operations; the analytics-export module was scheduled for inclusion at the next surveillance cycle and was not yet within the certified scope.
Witness Statement of Marcus Feld, Chief Technology Officer of Cobalt.
Our ISO 27001 certificate was valid for the contracted hosting services throughout, and our pre-contract materials reflected that certified scope.
The forecasting work used aggregated and de-identified datasets; to my knowledge it did not use identifiable Brightmarket customer records for training.
Incident Timeline and System Logs (extract), compiled 12 October 2025.

Access logging for the affected storage tier was incomplete for part of the incident window owing to a log-rotation gap; the number of records actually read cannot be determined from the available logs.

A security-group rule permitting inbound access from any address was present on a staging subnet; the change history needed to establish how long it had been in place had been truncated by retention limits.
Email thread — Cobalt Sales to Brightmarket Procurement, 5 December 2016 (pre-contract).
To answer your security questionnaire: the Platform is ISO 27001 certified and GDPR-compliant by design, and we would be happy to share our certificate.
Backup & Disaster-Recovery Runbook and Restore-Test Record, 15 July 2025.
Backups: hourly snapshots of the production data stores are taken and retained for 30 days, with daily off-region copies.
Restore test of 15 July 2025: a full restore from snapshot recovered 99.4% of the test dataset within the four-hour recovery-time objective.
The 0.6% not recovered relates to a known indexing gap in a legacy table flagged for decommissioning; it is unrelated to any specific incident.
Cookie / Consent Configuration Log — Brightmarket storefront, 1 September 2025.

A consent banner was active. In a sample of 1,000 sessions, a small number of analytics cookies were observed to fire before consent was recorded; the log does not attribute these to a specific script owner (Cobalt tag or the Customer's own tag manager).
DSAR & Erasure Register (extract) — Brightmarket, to 20 October 2025.
412 data-subject access and erasure requests were received in the period; all are recorded as closed within the statutory time limit. No request is recorded as refused or unactioned by the processor.
Procurement Due-Diligence Note — Brightmarket, 20 December 2016 (internal).
We verified Cobalt's ISO 27001 certificate and reviewed the certified scope before signing; we are satisfied to rely on it for the hosting services we are procuring.
Service Level Annex to the Master Subscription Agreement, between Brightmarket Retail GmbH and Cobalt Cloud Analytics Ltd.
The Platform has a monthly availability target of 99.9%.

This figure is a service-level target only and is not a warranty or guarantee of uptime; the Customer's sole and exclusive remedy for any failure to meet the target is the service credits set out in paragraph 4, and the Supplier shall have no liability in damages for unavailability.
Service credits are capped at 10% of the monthly fee for the affected month and are the only compensation payable for a missed availability target.
Outage Impact Assessment — Brightmarket / Cobalt analytics dashboard incident, October 2025.
The outage lasted approximately 30 hours, from 02:00 on 10 October 2025 to 08:00 on 11 October 2025.
Brightmarket's average daily gross revenue across the affected stores is approximately EUR 150,000; this figure is gross revenue, not margin.
The public storefront remained available throughout; only the internal analytics dashboard was affected, and the estimated gross-margin impact of the outage is approximately EUR 30,000.
From: Brightmarket IT. To: Cobalt Support. Sent: 25 October 2025, 14:10. Subject: Follow-up after the 10 October breach.
Following the breach earlier this month, please review the security-group configuration; treat this email as a formal warning that any recurrence will be escalated.
Remediation Ticket Register (extract) — Cobalt platform operations.
Ticket SEC-1187 was opened at 09:00 on 12 October 2025 and closed at 17:40 on 12 October 2025; the misconfiguration was remediated the same day.
Sub-processor Register (extract) — Cobalt Cloud Analytics Ltd.
The India-based sub-processor, Cobalt Analytics India Pvt Ltd, was de-listed and removed from the sub-processor register on 30 November 2023; no production personal data has been routed to it since.
Data-Flow Map (extract) — Brightmarket processing environments.
The India development sandbox processes synthetic and de-identified test data only; no EU customer personal data flows to the India environment.
Access Log (extract): an inbound session from a Mumbai (India) IP address, 203.0.113.7, reached the analytics tier on 12 October 2025.
On investigation, that session resolves to an EU-based engineer connecting through the company's Mumbai VPN egress node; the underlying access originated from within the EU.

paras.text: Data Processing Agreement — DRAFT v0.9. Marked 'subject to contract'; not executed.
Indemnity (draft): the Supplier shall indemnify the Customer without limit for any data-protection breach.
Data Processing Agreement — EXECUTED version, signed 1 March 2017.
Indemnity: the Supplier's indemnity for data-protection breaches is capped at the fees paid in the preceding 12 months; this executed Agreement supersedes any prior draft, including draft v0.9.
Encryption Configuration Note — Brightmarket / Cobalt platform.
Customer data at rest is encrypted with AES-256.
A legacy archive tier still uses 3DES; migration of that tier to AES-256 is scheduled for Q4 2025.
Expert Addendum — Dr Idris Vale, on encryption sufficiency.

I am unable to opine whether retaining 3DES on the legacy archive tier fell below the Article 32 standard; reasonable security experts may differ, and the answer turns on the residual risk of that specific tier.
Data Processing Agreement — clause 10 (assessments and assistance).
The Controller is responsible for carrying out any data protection impact assessment required by Article 35 GDPR; the Processor shall provide reasonable assistance under Article 28(3)(f).

chronology.event: Pre-contract sales email describes the Platform as 'ISO 27001 certified and GDPR-compliant by design'.
chronology.remarks: The representation underlying P1 was made — but see the audit scope (doc 27).

chronology.event: Brightmarket's own procurement note records it verified and relied on Cobalt's ISO 27001 certificate.
chronology.remarks: Own-goal cutting against 'the representation was false' (P1).

chronology.event: Parties enter the MSA, DPA and SOW; Cobalt appointed as Brightmarket's processor.
GDPR Addendum: references to Directive 95/46/EC replaced with the GDPR Chapter V regime.
chronology.remarks: Supersedes the data-transfer standard pleaded in P8.

chronology.event: International Transfer Annex adopts the 2021 SCCs (Dec (EU) 2021/914), replacing the 2010/87/EU clauses.
chronology.remarks: Second partial supersession; bears on the transfer mechanism (P8b).

chronology.event: Cobalt's acquisition of NorthStar notified and cleared; below EU Merger Regulation thresholds.
Internal Cobalt email: merchant datasets used to train a forecasting model without consent.
chronology.remarks: Admission underpinning P2 (contested by the CTO, doc 28).

chronology.event: Cobalt issues written security-hardening guidance: enable administrator MFA.
DR restore test recovers 99.4% of data within the recovery-time objective.
chronology.remarks: Contradicts P9 (no adequate backups); the 0.6% gap underlies P9b.

chronology.event: Penetration test finds a publicly reachable staging endpoint — production exposure unconfirmed.
Defect Log records the export module overstating multi-currency revenue.
Personal-data breach detected.
Cobalt notifies Brightmarket of the breach within 18 hours.
chronology.remarks: Processor's Art 33(2) duty discharged; supervisory-authority notice was the controller's (P4 allocation trap).

chronology.event: Brightmarket (controller) notifies the DPC — on day 6, late — putting affected data subjects at ~19,000.
chronology.remarks: Own-goal: contradicts P3 (2.3m) and shows the 72h duty was the controller's (P4).

chronology.event: Brightmarket's own internal security review finds its admins had disabled MFA.
DPC letter: notification was late; the processor had informed the controller without undue delay.
chronology.remarks: Reinforces the P4 allocation trap; controller/processor assessment ongoing.

chronology.event: Brightmarket issues proceedings (Particulars of Claim).
Joint forensic expert (Vale): ~14,000 records exfiltrated, not 2.3 million.
chronology.remarks: Contradicts P3; conflicts with the DPO's witness estimate.

chronology.event: Claimant's expert (Strand) agrees ~14,000 exfiltrated but argues ~1.9m 'exposed' and a Cobalt segmentation failing.
chronology.remarks: Dueling experts: drives the murk in P3b (exposure) and P5 (concurrent causation).

chronology.event: India sub-processor de-listed and removed from the register; no production data routed to it since.
Analytics-dashboard outage ends after ~30 hours; the public storefront was unaffected.
chronology.remarks: Pleaded 30 days / EUR 4.5m is a unit/scale trap (P16).

chronology.event: Misconfiguration remediated the same day it was ticketed (SEC-1187).
Post-breach follow-up email — mis-pleaded as a 25 September pre-breach warning.
chronology.remarks: Date trap underlying P17; the Claimant's own email.
